text.title: Year-End Planning, State Compliance, and AI-Agent Gotchas
text.summary: December action checklist to minimize tax liability, state nexus and sales tax considerations, and LLM limitations for tax work.

section.title: Year-End Planning

rule.statement: Reassess S-Corp election each December once SE-eligible income exceeds approximately $80–100K. The election can save $5–15K in self-employment tax annually at that income level.
rule.rationale: SE tax is 15.3% on net self-employment income. An S-Corp allows splitting income into salary (SE-taxable) and distributions (not SE-taxable), reducing the SE-taxable portion. The savings compound annually.

section.p: December year-end checklist: review annual profit and calculate current tax liability, max out retirement contributions (Solo 401(k) or SEP-IRA reduce taxable income dollar-for-dollar), purchase qualified equipment under Section 179 (deduct full cost in current year), pay outstanding business expenses to accelerate deductions, consider deferring income to January if expecting a lower-income year next year, calculate Q4 estimate due January 15.

section.title: State Tax Compliance

rule.statement: Determine nexus in each state where you have customers, employees, or business activity before exceeding sales thresholds. Selling SaaS into CA/NY/WA above $100K in revenue or 200 transactions in 12 months typically creates nexus and tax registration obligation.
rule.rationale: States have aggressive nexus enforcement and can assess back taxes plus penalties and interest retroactively. Early registration costs a few hours; retroactive compliance costs a lawyer and months of reconciliation.

section.p: Multi-state checklist: determine nexus in each state, register for state taxes where required, set up state quarterly estimates, track sales by state for reporting, file state returns separately from federal.

rule.statement: For SaaS businesses, check whether marketplace facilitator status applies before registering for direct sales tax collection. If you sell through a platform that collects and remits on your behalf, direct registration may be unnecessary.
rule.rationale: Marketplace facilitator treatment eliminates the compliance burden for sales made through covered platforms. Registering unnecessarily creates ongoing filing obligations without benefit.

antipattern.description: LLMs cite tax rules from prior years as current-year law.
antipattern.reason: Always require the agent to cite an IRS publication number and revision date. Standard deduction, mileage rate, HSA/401(k) limits change annually. Verify against IRS.gov before acting.

antipattern.description: Agents produce confident-sounding state nexus conclusions for jurisdictions the business has never operated in.
antipattern.reason: State nexus rules vary on dozens of dimensions (economic nexus thresholds, affiliate nexus, click-through nexus). Agents flatten this to single-rule reasoning. Require a CPA for any new-state nexus determination.

antipattern.description: Agent pastes full tax IDs, SSNs, EINs, or bank account numbers into prompts.
antipattern.reason: Any data in the prompt can appear in logs, context summaries, or API response metadata. Use a scrubber pipeline before any LLM call involving financial records.

antipattern.description: Agent drafts a response to an IRS notice without mandatory CPA sign-off.
antipattern.reason: IRS-facing communications trigger formal processes. An incorrect response can waive defenses or create new liability. Agents should only prepare a facts-and-timeline summary for the human and CPA to review.
